title: Classical Literature
intro: Questions on Classical Literature draw overwhelmingly from fifth-century Athenian drama and the Latin poetry of Augustan Rome. Aristophanes is the single most frequently asked author, with his surviving Old Comedy plays — Lysistrata, The Clouds, The Frogs, The Wasps, and Peace — each appearing as individual answerlines alongside his name. Greek tragedy is represented through the three canonical tragedians: Aeschylus (Agamemnon, The Persians), Sophocles (Antigone), and Euripides (Medea, The Bacchae), with mythological figures such as Orestes and Agamemnon recurring as common-link answerlines that span multiple plays and playwrights.

On the Roman side, comedy centers on Plautus and Terence, whose adaptations of Greek New Comedy include Plautus's Miles Gloriosus. Latin poetry is concentrated in the Augustan period, spanning Catullus's personal and invective verse, Ovid's Metamorphoses and Ars Amatoria, Propertius's love elegies, and Statius's later epic Thebaid. Prose fiction appears through Apuleius's The Golden Ass and Petronius's Satyricon, the two major surviving works of Roman narrative prose, while Seneca the Younger bridges Stoic philosophy and Latin tragedy.

Sappho and Pindar anchor the lyric tradition, with Sappho appearing under two distinct answerline forms reflecting her frequency in the canon. Recurring metadata tags — Old Comedy, Greek tragedy, Latin Literature, and Augustan Poetry — confirm that the unit's canon concentrates almost entirely in two eras: democratic Athens of the fifth century BCE and the literary flowering of Augustan Rome, with occasional Aristotelian literary theory (catharsis) supplying the analytical vocabulary used to discuss both.

sections.name: Greek Tragedy
sections.blurb: Athenian dramas of myth and suffering staged at civic festivals, from Aeschylus's Oresteia through Sophocles and Euripides.

entries.note: Euripides's sorceress who murders her own children to punish husband Jason for abandoning her for Glauce.
Earliest of the three great Athenian tragedians; wrote the Oresteia trilogy, The Persians, and Seven Against Thebes.
Mycenaean king who led the Greeks at Troy, murdered by wife Clytemnestra upon his return; Aeschylus's play opens the Oresteia.
Sophocles's heroine who defies Creon's edict by burying her brother Polynices, leading to her own death by suicide.
Son of Agamemnon and Clytemnestra who avenges his father's murder by killing his mother, then is pursued by the Furies.
Aeschylus's earliest surviving tragedy, dramatizing the Persian court's reaction to defeat at Salamis; the only extant historical Greek tragedy.
Youngest of the three great tragedians, known for psychologically complex women and religious skepticism; wrote Medea and The Bacchae.
Euripides's final tragedy, in which Dionysus punishes Pentheus for denying his divinity and is torn apart by frenzied maenads.

sections.name: Greek Comedy
sections.blurb: Aristophanes's surviving Old Comedy plays, blending topical satire, fantastical plots, and choral spectacle in fifth-century Athens.

entries.note: Athenian Old Comedy playwright whose only complete surviving plays include Lysistrata, The Clouds, The Frogs, The Wasps, and Peace.
Aristophanes's comedy in which farmer Trygaeus flies to Olympus on a giant dung beetle to rescue the goddess Peace.
Aristophanes's comedy satirizing Socrates and sophistic education, as Strepsiades sends his son Pheidippides to study at the Thinkery.
Aristophanes's comedy where Dionysus travels to the Underworld with slave Xanthias to judge a contest between Aeschylus and Euripides.
Aristophanes's comedy in which Athenian women withhold sex from their husbands to force an end to the Peloponnesian War.
Aristophanes's comedy satirizing Athenian litigiousness through Philocleon's obsession with jury service, restrained by his son Bdelycleon.
Slave of Dionysus in Aristophanes's The Frogs, comically switching roles and costumes with his master.

sections.name: Greek Lyric Poetry
sections.blurb: Personal and occasional Greek verse sung to music, spanning Sappho's intimate lyrics and Pindar's public victory odes.

entries.note: Archaic Greek poetess from Lesbos famed for intense personal lyrics of desire, often addressed to other women.
Archaic Greek lyric poet from Lesbos whose fragmentary verses on love and longing shaped later lyric poetry.
Greek lyric poet renowned for victory odes celebrating athletic triumphs at Olympia and other Panhellenic games.

sections.name: Roman Comedy
sections.blurb: Latin adaptations of Greek New Comedy for the Roman stage, built on stock characters, disguise, and clever slaves.

entries.note: Plautus's comedy about a vain soldier, Pyrgopolynices, outwitted by his clever slave Palaestrio over a captured mistress.
Roman comic playwright, a former slave from Carthage, who adapted Menander's Greek New Comedy into plays like The Brothers.
Roman comic playwright who adapted Greek New Comedy, writing Miles Gloriosus, Menaechmi, Pseudolus, and Amphitryon.

sections.name: Roman Poetry
sections.blurb: The Augustan-era flowering of Latin verse in elegy, epic, and lyric, from Catullus's invective to Ovid's mythological epic.

entries.note: Roman Neoteric poet known for passionate, obscene verse addressed to his lover Lesbia and translations of Sappho.
Augustan Roman poet of the Metamorphoses and Ars Amatoria, exiled by Augustus to Tomis on the Black Sea.
Full Roman name of Ovid, Augustan poet of the Metamorphoses and Ars Amatoria, later exiled to Tomis.
Flavian-era Roman epic poet who wrote the Thebaid, on the war of the Seven Against Thebes, and the Silvae.
Augustan Roman elegiac poet whose love poems address his mistress Cynthia; part of Maecenas's literary circle.

sections.name: Roman Prose and Satire
sections.blurb: Latin narrative prose that satirized contemporary Roman society through picaresque and fantastical plots.

entries.note: Apuleius's Latin novel, the only ancient Roman novel to survive complete; follows Lucius transformed into a donkey by magic.
Petronius's satirical Latin prose novel following Encolpius's misadventures, including the lavish banquet of the freedman Trimalchio.

sections.name: Roman Tragedy and Philosophy
sections.blurb: Seneca's Stoic philosophy and blood-soaked Latin tragedies reworking Greek myth for a Roman audience.

entries.note: Roman Stoic philosopher and tutor to Nero who wrote bloody Latin tragedies like Medea and Thyestes alongside philosophical letters.

sections.name: Literary Terms and Criticism
sections.blurb: Concepts from ancient literary theory used to analyze the effects and structure of classical drama and poetry.

entries.note: Aristotelian concept from the Poetics describing tragedy's purgation or purification of the audience's pity and fear.
